Industry standard for hydronic baseboard heating systems in North America. Web sources confirm 180°F is the standard design supply temperature for hot water loops, with historical precedent dating to industry standardization in late 1800s. Appropriate for commercial hydronic baseboard applications.

eQUEST/DOE-2 specification: HIR (Heat Input Ratio) is the inverse of efficiency. HIR of 1.04 corresponds to ~96% efficiency (1/1.04 ≈ 0.96), which matches condensing boiler specifications documented in eQUEST literature.

Industry standard: hydronic baseboard heating puts out approximately 600 BTU/hr per linear foot at 180°F supply temperature. 15,000 BTU/hr represents approximately 25 linear feet of baseboard, which is reasonable for a commercial perimeter zone.
This is a task-specified naming convention with no external source to verify against. Naming follows logical convention (Perimeter location + HW type + Loop designation).
This is a task-specified naming convention with no external source to verify against. Naming follows logical convention (Perimeter location + Boiler designation).
Verified in verifier.py: line 147 checks 'result.get("sim_ran", False)' as a requirement for passing (must be True). Also required for pass threshold.

privileged_info_summary: The hydronic_perimeter_heating_retrofit task uses the 4StoreyBuilding eQUEST model (verified .inp file), targeting four real top-floor perimeter zones (T.S31, T.E32, T.N33, T.W34 confirmed in model). Design specifications are consistent with industry standards: 180°F is the standard hot water supply temperature for North American hydronic baseboard systems, 1.04 HIR represents ~96% boiler efficiency (verified eQUEST specification), and 15,000 BTU/hr baseboard capacity is reasonable for commercial perimeter zones (~25 linear feet at standard output of 600 BTU/hr per linear foot). All building geometry and zone specifications match the actual eQUEST model file.
